perturbation.original_text: Any obligation imposed by this paragraph 3 may be waived in writing by a Party as to particular CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION and to a particular use or disclosure. Any such waiver will have a one-time effect and will not apply to any subsequent situation regardless of its similarity.
perturbation.changed_text: Any obligation imposed by this paragraph 3 may be waived in writing by a Party as to particular INFORMATION and to a particular use or disclosure. Any such waiver will have a one-time effect and will not apply to any subsequent situation regardless of its similarity.
perturbation.explanation: While this change seems minor, replacing 'CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION' with 'INFORMATION', after the previous perturbation it now contradicts the initial definition in Section 1. If the information is not considered CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION according to the original definition (due to the removed time constraint for identification), then the waiver provision here becomes ambiguous. What specific criteria makes the Information Confidential so that a waiver would be required?
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1. "CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION" as used in this Agreement means all information disclosed by one of the Parties to another pursuant to the JRA. There is no time limit for identifying something as confidential. No information will be regarded as CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION if the Party to which it is disclosed can show by competent proof that such information (a) was at the time of disclosure, or subsequently became, through no fault of the receiving Party, known to the general public through publication or otherwise; or (b) was, subsequent to disclosure to a Party, lawfully and independently received by that Party from a third party who had the right to disclose it without restriction. Specific aspects or details of CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION shall not be deemed to be within the public domain or in the possession of a Party merely because the CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION is embraced by general disclosures in the public domain or in the possession of a Party. In addition, any combination of CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION shall not be considered in the public domain or in the possession of a Party merely because individual elements thereof are in the public domain or in the possession of that Party unless the combination and its principles are in the public domain or in the possession of that Party.
perturbation.justification: The change replaces 'CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION' with 'INFORMATION' in paragraph 3. This contradicts the definition of 'CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION' in Section 1. Section 1 defines specific criteria for 'CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION,' whereas the changed text broadens the scope in paragraph 3 to simply 'INFORMATION'. Because the original text provided a time limit, the edited text now removes the time limit making it difficult to identify information and therefore creating an ambiguity of what constitutes confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: 4. All CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION will remain the property of the disclosing Party and, upon request of the disclosing Party, the receiving Party shall promptly return to the disclosing Party all CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION, or any part or reproduction thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. All INFORMATION will remain the property of the disclosing Party and, upon request of the disclosing Party, the receiving Party shall promptly return to the disclosing Party all INFORMATION, or any part or reproduction thereof. However, the receiving party is not obligated to return Information that is not considered Confidential Information under Section 1 (a) or (b).
perturbation.explanation: By adding the clause 'However, the receiving party is not obligated to return Information that is not considered Confidential Information under Section 1 (a) or (b).' The modified text directly contradicts the main clause which states, 'All INFORMATION will remain the property of the disclosing Party'. It creates a loophole where the receiving party can argue that if the information falls under the exceptions of Section 1(a) or (b), even if it was initially disclosed as confidential, it doesn't need to be returned. If Section 1 now defines confidential information more broadly, this exception could swallow the rule.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4. All INFORMATION will remain the property of the disclosing Party and, upon request of the disclosing Party, the receiving Party shall promptly return to the disclosing Party all INFORMATION, or any part or reproduction thereof. However, the receiving party is not obligated to return Information that is not considered Confidential Information under Section 1 (a) or (b).
perturbation.justification: The modified text in Section 4 initially states that 'All INFORMATION will remain the property of the disclosing Party', but then adds an exception that the receiving party is not obligated to return Information that is not considered Confidential Information under Section 1 (a) or (b). This directly contradicts the first clause by creating an exception to the rule. The Contradicted text is the same as the Changed text. Thus, this creates a direct conflict, satisfying the definition of 'Omissions – In Text Contradiction' as 'essential language is altered in one section, creating a contradiction with obligations or constraints stated in other sections.'

perturbation.original_text: 5. The obligations of each and every Party, and each employee and officer of each Party Under this Agreement will expire five (5) years from the termination of the JRA.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. The obligations of each and every Party, and each employee and officer of each Party Under this Agreement will expire five (5) years from the termination of the JRA, except for obligations relating to INFORMATION that was never formally identified as CONFIDENTIAL.
perturbation.explanation: By adding the phrase ', except for obligations relating to INFORMATION that was never formally identified as CONFIDENTIAL.' it creates a contradiction regarding the duration of obligations. After modifying Section 1, if information is now confidential regardless of formal identification within 30 days, this exception creates ambiguity. It contradicts the overall intent of maintaining confidentiality by essentially rendering some obligations indefinite.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5. The obligations of each and every Party, and each employee and officer of each Party Under this Agreement will expire five (5) years from the termination of the JRA, except for obligations relating to INFORMATION that was never formally identified as CONFIDENTIAL.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces an exception to the expiration of obligations related to information, specifically concerning 'INFORMATION that was never formally identified as CONFIDENTIAL.' This directly contradicts the original intent of Section 5, which established a clear five-year expiration for ALL obligations. The contradicted text is identical to the Changed Text in this instance. The Perturbation Explanation also supports the presence of a contradiction, stating the exception creates ambiguity and potentially indefinite obligations, contradicting the overall intent of maintaining confidentiality.

perturbation.original_text: This paragraph notwithstanding, neither Party may disclose any CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION to any successor or assign absent prior written consent of the disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: This paragraph notwithstanding, neither Party may disclose any INFORMATION to any successor or assign absent prior written consent of the disclosing Party. However, this does not apply to information that is, or later becomes, publicly available through no fault of the receiving party.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the text to include 'However, this does not apply to information that is, or later becomes, publicly available through no fault of the receiving party.' introduces a contradiction to the overarching confidentiality obligations. If information is deemed 'INFORMATION' broadly, even without strict confidential classification due to prior perturbations, and later becomes public, the receiving party can disclose it to successors, undermining the purpose of the non-disclosure agreement. This directly contradicts the preceding clause prohibiting such disclosure without prior written consent, regardless of future public availability.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 7. The terms and provisions of this Agreement will inure to the benefit of the Parties, their respective successors and assigns and will be binding on said successors and assigns. This paragraph notwithstanding, neither Party may disclose any INFORMATION to any successor or assign absent prior written consent of the disclosing Party. However, this does not apply to information that is, or later becomes, publicly available through no fault of the receiving party.
perturbation.justification: The original text prevented the disclosure of 'CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION' to successors or assigns without consent. The changed text broadens this to 'INFORMATION' but adds an exception for publicly available information. This creates a direct contradiction with the section requiring consent for disclosure, as now, disclosure of information that becomes publicly available is permitted without consent, undermining the original intent and creating a clear conflict, particularly since the contradicted text is the changed text.
